description:
Bug report: Our INI config parser silently strips parts of quoted values that contain semicolons. In INI, `;` starts a comment, but a `;` inside a double-quoted value is literal.

For example, the input line:
  `path = "C:\\tools;C:\\bin" ; primary path`
should parse to key=`path` and value=`C:\tools;C:\bin`. Our parser returns value=`C:\tools` because it strips the comment before looking at quotes.

Here is the current implementation that has a bug:

```python
def parse_ini_line(line):
    """Parse one INI line. Return (key, value) or None for blank/comment.
    Supports double-quoted values which may contain ';' and '='.
    A ';' outside of quotes starts a comment.
    """
    line = line.split(';', 1)[0].rstrip()
    if not line.strip() or line.lstrip().startswith('['):
        return None
    if '=' not in line:
        return None
    k, v = line.split('=', 1)
    k = k.strip()
    v = v.strip()
    if len(v) >= 2 and v[0] == '"' and v[-1] == '"':
        v = v[1:-1]
    return k, v
```

Please fix the bug in `parse_ini_line`.
test_code:
# Test 1: Plain key/value
assert parse_ini_line('name = foo') == ('name', 'foo')

# Test 2: Inline comment after unquoted value
assert parse_ini_line('name = foo ; a comment') == ('name', 'foo')

# Test 3: Semicolon inside quoted value must be preserved
res = parse_ini_line('path = "C:\\tools;C:\\bin" ; primary path')
assert res == ('path', 'C:\\tools;C:\\bin'), f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: Equals sign inside quoted value must be preserved as part of value
res = parse_ini_line('expr = "a=b=c"')
assert res == ('expr', 'a=b=c'), f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: Quoted empty string
assert parse_ini_line('empty = ""') == ('empty', '')

# Test 6: Section headers return None
assert parse_ini_line('[section]') is None
assert parse_ini_line('   [s]  ') is None

# Test 7: Full-line comment returns None
assert parse_ini_line('; this is a comment') is None

# Test 8: Blank returns None
assert parse_ini_line('   ') is None

# Test 9: Whitespace preserved inside quotes, trimmed outside
res = parse_ini_line('greeting =   " hello world "   ; x')
assert res == ('greeting', ' hello world '), f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 10: Semicolon outside quotes is still a comment
res = parse_ini_line('mode = fast ; mode = slow')
assert res == ('mode', 'fast'), f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 11: Value with semicolon in quotes followed by comment
res = parse_ini_line('list = "a;b;c" ; three items')
assert res == ('list', 'a;b;c'), f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 12: Missing '=' returns None
assert parse_ini_line('no_equals_here') is None

print('All parse_ini_line tests passed!')
common_bugs: Stripping comments via a naive split(';') before considering quote state
Only checking for quotes at start/end of the value after comment-strip has already truncated the line
Not tracking whether the cursor is inside a quoted span when scanning for the comment delimiter
Trimming whitespace inside the quoted value by calling .strip() on the unquoted content

description:
Bug report: Our HTTP header parser drops continuation lines. Per RFC 7230 §3.2.4, a header field-value that starts on the next line with leading whitespace is an obsolete 'line folding' continuation of the previous header.

For example, the raw header block:
  `X-Long: value-part-one,\r\n value-part-two`
should parse to a single header `X-Long` with value `value-part-one, value-part-two` (the CRLF + leading whitespace collapses to a single space). Our parser returns `X-Long: value-part-one,` and a second bogus header whose name is empty.

Here is the current implementation that has a bug:

```python
def parse_headers(raw):
    """Parse an HTTP header block (without the request line, without the
    terminating blank line) into a list of (name, value) tuples.
    Header names are case-preserved. Values are stripped of surrounding
    whitespace. Continuation lines (obs-fold) must be supported.
    """
    headers = []
    for line in raw.split('\r\n'):
        if not line:
            continue
        name, _, value = line.partition(':')
        headers.append((name, value.strip()))
    return headers
```

Please fix the bug in `parse_headers`.
test_code:
# Test 1: Simple single header
res = parse_headers('Host: example.com')
assert res == [('Host', 'example.com')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 2: Two headers separated by CRLF
res = parse_headers('A: 1\r\nB: 2')
assert res == [('A', '1'), ('B', '2')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 3: Obs-fold continuation with a space
res = parse_headers('X-Long: value-part-one,\r\n value-part-two')
assert res == [('X-Long', 'value-part-one, value-part-two')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: Obs-fold continuation with a tab
res = parse_headers('Subject: Re:\r\n\tan important matter')
assert res == [('Subject', 'Re: an important matter')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: Multiple continuation lines
res = parse_headers('Trace: a,\r\n b,\r\n c')
assert res == [('Trace', 'a, b, c')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 6: Continuation followed by new header
res = parse_headers('X: alpha\r\n beta\r\nY: gamma')
assert res == [('X', 'alpha beta'), ('Y', 'gamma')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 7: Header with colon in value
res = parse_headers('Host: example.com:8080')
assert res == [('Host', 'example.com:8080')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 8: Case preservation of header names
res = parse_headers('Content-Type: text/plain')
assert res == [('Content-Type', 'text/plain')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 9: Leading whitespace on the very first line is NOT a continuation
# (nothing to continue); such a line should be ignored or produce no header.
res = parse_headers(' leading-space-line\r\nA: 1')
# Acceptable behaviours: either skip the orphan fold, or treat as no headers prior.
assert ('A', '1') in res, f'Got {res!r}'
assert all(name != '' for name, _ in res), f'Got empty header name in {res!r}'

# Test 10: Multiple headers, no folding
res = parse_headers('A: 1\r\nB: 2\r\nC: 3')
assert res == [('A', '1'), ('B', '2'), ('C', '3')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 11: Continuation preserves internal commas/semicolons
res = parse_headers('Accept: text/html,\r\n application/json; q=0.9')
assert res == [('Accept', 'text/html, application/json; q=0.9')], f'Got {res!r}'

print('All parse_headers tests passed!')
common_bugs: Treating every CRLF as a header boundary without checking whether the next line begins with SP or HTAB
Collapsing obs-fold newlines without inserting the replacement single space
Emitting a header whose name is empty because partition(':') returned '' for the colonless continuation line
Merging continuation whitespace into the value instead of stripping it back to a single separator space

description:
Bug report: Our shell-style argument tokenizer over-unescapes backslashes inside single-quoted strings. In POSIX shell, inside single quotes a backslash is literal — `'\n'` is the two characters `\` and `n`. Only inside double quotes (and outside quotes) is `\` an escape.

For example:
  `echo 'a\nb'` should tokenize to `['echo', 'a\nb']` (four chars in second token).
  `echo "a\nb"` should tokenize to `['echo', 'a\nb']` as well — in POSIX `\n` inside double quotes is NOT an escape because `n` is not one of the few special chars (`$`, `` ` ``, `"`, `\`, newline), so the backslash is preserved literally.
  `echo "a\"b"` should tokenize to `['echo', 'a"b']` (the `\"` IS an escape in double quotes).
  `echo a\ b` should tokenize to `['echo', 'a b']` (escaped space joins tokens).

Our tokenizer collapses `\` + any char to the char in all contexts, which breaks single-quoted strings.

Here is the current implementation that has a bug:

```python
def shell_tokenize(s):
    """Split a shell-like command into tokens.
    - Double quotes: preserve literal whitespace; `\\` escapes $, `, ", \\, and newline only.
    - Single quotes: everything is literal; no escapes at all.
    - Outside quotes: `\\` escapes the next character (including whitespace).
    Adjacent quoted/unquoted pieces are concatenated into one token.
    """
    tokens = []
    buf = []
    i = 0
    in_single = False
    in_double = False
    in_token = False
    n = len(s)
    while i < n:
        ch = s[i]
        if ch == '\\' and i + 1 < n:
            buf.append(s[i + 1])
            i += 2
            in_token = True
            continue
        if ch == "'" and not in_double:
            in_single = not in_single
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch == '"' and not in_single:
            in_double = not in_double
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch.isspace() and not in_single and not in_double:
            if in_token:
                tokens.append(''.join(buf))
                buf = []
                in_token = False
            i += 1
            continue
        buf.append(ch)
        in_token = True
        i += 1
    if in_single or in_double:
        raise ValueError('Unterminated quote')
    if in_token:
        tokens.append(''.join(buf))
    return tokens
```

Please fix the bugs in `shell_tokenize`.
test_code:
# Test 1: Simple whitespace split
assert shell_tokenize('echo hello world') == ['echo', 'hello', 'world']

# Test 2: Double-quoted string preserves spaces
assert shell_tokenize('echo "hello world"') == ['echo', 'hello world']

# Test 3: Single quotes are fully literal
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'a\\nb'")
assert res == ['echo', 'a\\nb'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: Backslash-space outside quotes escapes space
res = shell_tokenize('echo a\\ b')
assert res == ['echo', 'a b'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: Double-quoted backslash+n is literal (n is not special)
res = shell_tokenize('echo "a\\nb"')
assert res == ['echo', 'a\\nb'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 6: Double-quoted backslash+double-quote is escape
res = shell_tokenize('echo "a\\"b"')
assert res == ['echo', 'a"b'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 7: Double-quoted backslash+dollar is escape
res = shell_tokenize('echo "\\$var"')
assert res == ['echo', '$var'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 8: Mixed single+double quoted concatenation forms one token
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'hello'\" world\"")
assert res == ['echo', 'hello world'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 9: Adjacent single+unquoted concat
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'pre'fix")
assert res == ['echo', 'prefix'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 10: Single quotes containing a double quote
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'a\"b'")
assert res == ['echo', 'a"b'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 11: Empty quoted string still creates an empty token
res = shell_tokenize("cmd '' arg")
assert res == ['cmd', '', 'arg'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 12: Unterminated quote raises
try:
    shell_tokenize("echo 'never closed")
    assert False, 'Expected ValueError'
except ValueError:
    pass

print('All shell_tokenize tests passed!')
common_bugs: Applying the outside-quotes `\\`-escapes-next-char rule universally, including inside single quotes where the backslash is literal
Applying the outside-quotes escape rule inside double quotes where only a specific subset of characters are escapable
Not recognising empty quoted strings `''` or `\"\"` as producing an empty-string token
Forgetting that adjacent quoted/unquoted spans concatenate into a single token rather than splitting

description:
Bug report: Our query-string parser mishandles repeated keys and percent/plus decoding. For `a=1&a=2&a=3`, the result should be `{'a': ['1', '2', '3']}` (or equivalently a dict with list values when keys repeat). Our parser returns `{'a': '3'}`, silently overwriting.

Also:
  - `+` must decode to a space in values AND keys.
  - `%2B` must decode to a literal `+`.
  - A bare key with no `=` (e.g. `flag`) should produce value `''` (empty string), not be dropped.
  - An empty value (e.g. `k=`) should produce `''`.

Here is the current implementation that has a bug:

```python
def parse_qs(qs):
    """Parse an application/x-www-form-urlencoded query string.
    Return a dict: str -> list[str] if the key appears more than once,
    or str -> str for single-occurrence keys. Apply + -> ' ' and %XX decoding
    to both keys and values.
    """
    from urllib.parse import unquote
    result = {}
    if qs.startswith('?'):
        qs = qs[1:]
    if not qs:
        return result
    for part in qs.split('&'):
        if '=' in part:
            k, v = part.split('=', 1)
        else:
            continue
        k = unquote(k)
        v = unquote(v)
        result[k] = v
    return result
```

Please fix the bugs in `parse_qs`.
test_code:
# Test 1: Single key-value
assert parse_qs('a=1') == {'a': '1'}

# Test 2: Two distinct keys
assert parse_qs('a=1&b=2') == {'a': '1', 'b': '2'}

# Test 3: Repeated key collects list
res = parse_qs('a=1&a=2&a=3')
assert res == {'a': ['1', '2', '3']}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: + decodes to space in value
res = parse_qs('q=hello+world')
assert res == {'q': 'hello world'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: %2B decodes to literal +
res = parse_qs('op=%2B')
assert res == {'op': '+'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 6: Percent-encoded key
res = parse_qs('a%20b=1')
assert res == {'a b': '1'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 7: Bare key with no '='
res = parse_qs('flag&a=1')
assert res == {'flag': '', 'a': '1'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 8: Empty value
res = parse_qs('k=')
assert res == {'k': ''}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 9: Leading '?' allowed
res = parse_qs('?a=1&b=2')
assert res == {'a': '1', 'b': '2'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 10: Empty input
assert parse_qs('') == {}
assert parse_qs('?') == {}

# Test 11: Mixed repeated and unique
res = parse_qs('a=1&b=x&a=2')
assert res == {'a': ['1', '2'], 'b': 'x'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 12: + inside key too
res = parse_qs('first+name=jane')
assert res == {'first name': 'jane'}, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 13: Both + and %XX in same value
res = parse_qs('s=a%20b+c')
assert res == {'s': 'a b c'}, f'Got {res!r}'

print('All parse_qs tests passed!')
common_bugs: Overwriting the dict entry for a repeated key rather than promoting the value to a list
Using only unquote() instead of unquote_plus() (or equivalent manual handling) so '+' stays literal
Dropping bare keys (no '=' present) instead of treating them as empty-string values
Not handling the case where the query starts with a leading '?'

description:
Bug report: Our natural-sort comparator orders filenames incorrectly. It uses lexicographic comparison over the whole string, so `file10` sorts before `file2`.

Natural sort should compare consecutive runs of digits as integers, and consecutive runs of non-digits lexicographically. Leading zeros inside a digit run should NOT affect the numeric value (`file02` and `file2` have equal digit-values, so the shorter one sorts first as a tiebreaker).

Expected ordering for `['file1', 'file10', 'file2', 'file02', 'file20']`:
  `['file1', 'file02', 'file2', 'file10', 'file20']`

Here is the current implementation that has a bug:

```python
def natural_key(s):
    """Return a key tuple for natural sorting.
    Runs of digits compare as integers; runs of non-digits compare as strings.
    On integer-tied digit runs, the shorter run sorts first.
    """
    import re
    parts = re.split(r'(\d+)', s)
    key = []
    for p in parts:
        if p.isdigit():
            key.append(int(p))
        else:
            key.append(p)
    return key
```

`natural_key` is used as a key function: `sorted(items, key=natural_key)`.

Please fix the bugs in `natural_key` so that the expected orderings below all hold.
test_code:
# Test 1: Basic numeric suffix ordering
assert sorted(['file1', 'file10', 'file2'], key=natural_key) == ['file1', 'file2', 'file10']

# Test 2: Leading zeros tiebreak — shorter run sorts first on equal int value
res = sorted(['file02', 'file2'], key=natural_key)
assert res == ['file2', 'file02'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 3: Full mixed ordering
res = sorted(['file1', 'file10', 'file2', 'file02', 'file20'], key=natural_key)
assert res == ['file1', 'file2', 'file02', 'file10', 'file20'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: Multiple numeric sections
res = sorted(['v1.2', 'v1.10', 'v1.2.1', 'v1.2.0', 'v2.0'], key=natural_key)
assert res == ['v1.2', 'v1.2.0', 'v1.2.1', 'v1.10', 'v2.0'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: Tie on both int value and length => compare by original string position
assert sorted(['a1', 'a1'], key=natural_key) == ['a1', 'a1']

# Test 6: Pure text
assert sorted(['banana', 'apple', 'cherry'], key=natural_key) == ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

# Test 7: Pure numbers (as strings)
res = sorted(['100', '20', '3'], key=natural_key)
assert res == ['3', '20', '100'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 8: Leading zero on a pure number
res = sorted(['03', '3', '30'], key=natural_key)
assert res == ['3', '03', '30'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 9: Letters and digits interleaved
res = sorted(['a10b2', 'a10b10', 'a2b1'], key=natural_key)
assert res == ['a2b1', 'a10b2', 'a10b10'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 10: Number at start
res = sorted(['10a', '2a', '1a'], key=natural_key)
assert res == ['1a', '2a', '10a'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 11: Number only vs number with suffix — numbers first
res = sorted(['2', '2a'], key=natural_key)
assert res == ['2', '2a'], f'Got {res!r}'

print('All natural_key tests passed!')
common_bugs: Failing to construct a tuple whose elements are comparable when mixed with strings vs ints (TypeError on heterogeneous keys)
Losing the leading-zero information so '02' and '2' compare equal and can swap under a non-stable tiebreak
Not starting the key with a placeholder for the 'empty leading segment' case when the string begins with a digit, causing index misalignment when comparing '2a' vs 'a2'
Treating leading zeros as producing a longer string tiebreaker on the wrong side (longer-first instead of shorter-first)

description:
Bug report: Our CIDR range overlap checker returns wrong results at range boundaries and for nested networks. Two CIDR networks overlap iff one contains the other's network address, i.e. (start1 <= end2) AND (start2 <= end1), treating addresses as 32-bit unsigned ints.

Our code uses OR instead of AND in one place, and miscomputes the broadcast address for the case where the prefix length equals 32 (single host).

For example:
  `cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.128/25')` should return True (contained).
  `cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.1.0/24')` should return False (adjacent, non-overlapping).
  `cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.5/32', '10.0.0.0/24')` should return True.

Here is the current implementation that has bugs:

```python
def _parse_cidr(cidr):
    ip_str, _, plen_str = cidr.partition('/')
    plen = int(plen_str)
    octets = [int(o) for o in ip_str.split('.')]
    ip = (octets[0] << 24) | (octets[1] << 16) | (octets[2] << 8) | octets[3]
    mask = ((1 << 32) - 1) << (32 - plen) & 0xFFFFFFFF
    start = ip & mask
    end = start + (1 << (32 - plen))
    return start, end

def cidr_overlaps(a, b):
    """Return True iff CIDR ranges a and b share at least one address."""
    a_start, a_end = _parse_cidr(a)
    b_start, b_end = _parse_cidr(b)
    return a_start <= b_end or b_start <= a_end
```

Please fix the bugs so all the tests below pass.
test_code:
# Test 1: Identical networks overlap
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.0/24') is True

# Test 2: One strictly contained in the other
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.128/25') is True
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.128/25', '10.0.0.0/24') is True

# Test 3: Adjacent non-overlapping
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.1.0/24') is False

# Test 4: Disjoint
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '192.168.0.0/24') is False

# Test 5: /32 single host inside a /24
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.5/32', '10.0.0.0/24') is True
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.5/32') is True

# Test 6: /32 vs /32 same address
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.5/32', '10.0.0.5/32') is True

# Test 7: /32 vs /32 different address
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.5/32', '10.0.0.6/32') is False

# Test 8: /0 covers everything
assert cidr_overlaps('0.0.0.0/0', '8.8.8.8/32') is True
assert cidr_overlaps('8.8.8.8/32', '0.0.0.0/0') is True

# Test 9: Boundary — last address of first == first address of second
# 10.0.0.0/25 covers .0-.127; 10.0.0.128/25 covers .128-.255 — disjoint
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/25', '10.0.0.128/25') is False

# Test 10: Boundary — last address of first == first address of second, but overlapping by 1
# 10.0.0.0/24 covers .0-.255; 10.0.0.255/32 is the last address — overlap
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.255/32') is True

# Test 11: Non-canonical host bits tolerated
# 10.0.0.5/24 has host bits set; it should be treated as the /24 network
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.5/24', '10.0.0.200/32') is True

# Test 12: Far apart
assert cidr_overlaps('1.1.1.0/24', '255.255.255.0/24') is False

print('All cidr_overlaps tests passed!')
common_bugs: Using OR instead of AND when checking the two-sided overlap condition (start1 <= end2) AND (start2 <= end1)
Treating `end` as inclusive in one place and exclusive in another, leading to off-by-one at adjacency
Computing broadcast as `start + 2^(32-plen)` (exclusive) and then comparing as if it were the last valid address (inclusive)
Integer overflow when plen is 0 and shifting by 32 on a 32-bit-masked value

test_code:
s = SkipList()

# Test 1: Empty skip list
assert len(s) == 0
assert s.contains(5) is False
assert s.count(5) == 0

# Test 2: Insert and contains
s.insert(10, level=0)
assert s.contains(10) is True
assert len(s) == 1
assert s.count(10) == 1

# Test 3: Duplicates stored
s.insert(10, level=1)
s.insert(10, level=0)
assert len(s) == 3, f'Expected 3, got {len(s)}'
assert s.count(10) == 3, f'Expected count 3, got {s.count(10)}'

# Test 4: Mixed keys with duplicates
s.insert(5, level=2)
s.insert(20, level=0)
s.insert(5, level=0)
assert len(s) == 5, f'Expected 5, got {len(s)}'
assert s.count(5) == 2
assert s.count(10) == 3
assert s.count(20) == 1

# Test 5: Remove peels off one at a time
assert s.remove(10) is True
assert s.count(10) == 2, f'Expected 2 after remove, got {s.count(10)}'
assert s.remove(10) is True
assert s.remove(10) is True
assert s.count(10) == 0
assert s.contains(10) is False
assert s.remove(10) is False  # already gone

# Test 6: Sorted iteration over remaining multiset
keys = []
cur = s.head.next[0]
while cur is not None:
    keys.append(cur.key)
    cur = cur.next[0]
assert keys == [5, 5, 20], f'Got {keys!r}'

# Test 7: Insert then remove a level-lifted node retains ordering
s2 = SkipList()
for k, lvl in [(3, 0), (1, 2), (2, 1), (1, 0), (4, 0), (2, 0)]:
    s2.insert(k, level=lvl)
keys = []
cur = s2.head.next[0]
while cur is not None:
    keys.append(cur.key)
    cur = cur.next[0]
assert keys == [1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4], f'Got {keys!r}'
assert s2.count(1) == 2
assert s2.count(2) == 2

# Test 8: Remove a non-existent key
assert s2.remove(99) is False
assert len(s2) == 6

print('All SkipList tests passed!')
common_bugs: Short-circuiting insert when a node with the same key already exists, dropping duplicates silently
Relinking the upper lanes of an unrelated earlier node because the `update` array was captured before the equal-key check rather than scanning `< key` strictly
Using `<=` instead of `<` in the lane-walk predicate so walks overshoot past equal keys
Not handling the level-grow path (`level > self.level`) correctly because the `update` array only had entries up to the old `self.level`

test_code:
seeds = ['s0', 's1', 's2', 's3']

# Test 1: Fresh filter says contains is False and estimate is 0
bf = CountingBloom(128, seeds)
assert bf.contains('alpha') is False
assert bf.estimate() == 0

# Test 2: Insert then contains
bf.insert('alpha')
assert bf.contains('alpha') is True
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 3: Insert another distinct item
bf.insert('beta')
assert bf.contains('beta') is True
assert bf.estimate() == 2

# Test 4: Remove present item makes it absent (assuming no spurious collisions)
bf.remove('alpha')
assert bf.contains('alpha') is False
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 5: Remove absent item is a no-op — counters must not change
snapshot = list(bf.counters)
bf.remove('never_inserted')
assert bf.counters == snapshot, 'remove() of absent item must not modify counters'
assert bf.contains('beta') is True, 'beta must still be present after a no-op remove'
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 6: No counter can ever be negative
for c in bf.counters:
    assert c >= 0, f'Found negative counter: {c}'

# Test 7: Insert duplicates and remove them individually
bf2 = CountingBloom(256, seeds)
for _ in range(5):
    bf2.insert('gamma')
assert bf2.contains('gamma') is True
assert bf2.estimate() == 5
for _ in range(5):
    bf2.remove('gamma')
assert bf2.contains('gamma') is False
assert bf2.estimate() == 0
assert all(c >= 0 for c in bf2.counters)

# Test 8: Removing one more time does nothing
bf2.remove('gamma')
assert bf2.estimate() == 0
assert all(c >= 0 for c in bf2.counters)

# Test 9: Independent items are preserved after absent-remove
bf3 = CountingBloom(1024, seeds)
for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']:
    bf3.insert(x)
before = [bf3.contains(x) for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']]
assert all(before)
bf3.remove('z')  # z was never inserted
after = [bf3.contains(x) for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']]
assert after == before, 'absent-remove must not affect other items'

print('All CountingBloom tests passed!')
common_bugs: Decrementing counters without first checking contains(item)
Checking contains() but still decrementing on every slot even when the check failed
Capping counters at 0 via clamping but still having modified them, which changes which other items the filter reports as present
Letting estimate() return a negative number when counters go below zero

test_code:
# Test 1: Empty-tree sum is 0
st = SegTree(8)
assert st.query(0, 8) == 0

# Test 2: Single-range add, sum equals span * v
st.update(0, 4, 3)
assert st.query(0, 4) == 12
assert st.query(4, 8) == 0
assert st.query(0, 8) == 12

# Test 3: Overlapping update + partial query — this exposes missing pushdown
st.update(2, 6, 5)
# indices 0,1 have 3; 2,3 have 3+5=8; 4,5 have 5; 6,7 have 0
assert st.query(2, 4) == 16, f'Expected 16, got {st.query(2, 4)}'
assert st.query(0, 2) == 6
assert st.query(4, 6) == 10
assert st.query(6, 8) == 0
assert st.query(0, 8) == 32

# Test 4: Many stacked range-adds
st2 = SegTree(10)
for (l, r, v) in [(0, 5, 1), (3, 8, 2), (2, 7, 4), (1, 9, 1)]:
    st2.update(l, r, v)
# Compute expected via brute force
expected = [0] * 10
for (l, r, v) in [(0, 5, 1), (3, 8, 2), (2, 7, 4), (1, 9, 1)]:
    for i in range(l, r):
        expected[i] += v
assert st2.query(0, 10) == sum(expected)
for i in range(10):
    assert st2.query(i, i + 1) == expected[i], f'index {i}: got {st2.query(i, i + 1)}, expected {expected[i]}'
assert st2.query(2, 7) == sum(expected[2:7])
assert st2.query(3, 8) == sum(expected[3:8])

# Test 5: Zero-length range is a no-op
st3 = SegTree(5)
st3.update(2, 2, 100)
assert st3.query(0, 5) == 0
assert st3.query(2, 2) == 0

# Test 6: Single-element tree
st4 = SegTree(1)
st4.update(0, 1, 7)
assert st4.query(0, 1) == 7
st4.update(0, 1, -3)
assert st4.query(0, 1) == 4

# Test 7: Stress sequence of alternating queries and updates
import random
random.seed(42)
n = 20
st5 = SegTree(n)
arr = [0] * n
for _ in range(200):
    op = random.choice(['u', 'q'])
    l = random.randint(0, n - 1)
    r = random.randint(l + 1, n)
    if op == 'u':
        v = random.randint(-5, 5)
        st5.update(l, r, v)
        for i in range(l, r):
            arr[i] += v
    else:
        assert st5.query(l, r) == sum(arr[l:r]), f'mismatch at query({l},{r})'

print('All SegTree tests passed!')
common_bugs: Skipping the pushdown of the lazy tag before recursing into children on a partial-overlap update or query
Pushing down the tag but not applying it to both children before descending
Propagating the lazy tag to children but failing to clear it on the parent, causing double-application later
Incorrectly computing the applied delta as `v` instead of `(r - l) * v` so the subtree sum doesn't match

test_code:
# Test 1: Length comparator (contradicts lex compare on these strings)
h = Heap(lambda a, b: len(a) - len(b))
for s in ['ccc', 'a', 'bb', 'dddd']:
    h.push(s)
out = [h.pop() for _ in range(4)]
assert out == ['a', 'bb', 'ccc', 'dddd'], f'Got {out!r}'

# Test 2: Max-heap via reversed comparator
h = Heap(lambda a, b: b - a)
for x in [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]:
    h.push(x)
out = [h.pop() for _ in range(8)]
assert out == [9, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1], f'Got {out!r}'

# Test 3: Tuple comparator (second element only)
h = Heap(lambda a, b: a[1] - b[1])
pairs = [(0, 3), (1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 0)]
for p in pairs:
    h.push(p)
assert [h.pop() for _ in range(4)] == [(3, 0), (1, 1), (2, 2), (0, 3)]

# Test 4: Stable FIFO on ties
h = Heap(lambda a, b: (len(a) - len(b)))
for s in ['xx', 'aa', 'bb', 'c']:
    h.push(s)
assert [h.pop() for _ in range(4)] == ['c', 'xx', 'aa', 'bb']

# Test 5: Peek doesn't remove
h = Heap(lambda a, b: a - b)
h.push(2); h.push(1); h.push(3)
assert h.peek() == 1
assert len(h) == 3
assert h.pop() == 1
assert h.peek() == 2

# Test 6: Empty heap raises on pop
h = Heap(lambda a, b: a - b)
try:
    h.pop()
    assert False, 'Expected IndexError'
except IndexError:
    pass

# Test 7: Comparator that treats everything as equal — pure FIFO
h = Heap(lambda a, b: 0)
for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']:
    h.push(x)
assert [h.pop() for _ in range(4)] == ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']

# Test 8: Random stress against sorted() with the same comparator
import random, functools
random.seed(7)
for trial in range(20):
    cmp = lambda a, b: (a % 7) - (b % 7)
    data = [random.randint(0, 100) for _ in range(50)]
    h = Heap(cmp)
    for x in data:
        h.push(x)
    got = [h.pop() for _ in range(len(data))]
    want = sorted(data, key=functools.cmp_to_key(cmp))
    assert got == want, f'trial {trial}: got {got}, want {want}'

print('All Heap tests passed!')
common_bugs: Comparing `self.data[left] < self.data[smallest]` (native tuple compare) instead of calling `self._cmp(left, smallest) < 0`
Calling `self.compare(...)` directly in sift_down, bypassing the insertion-order tiebreak needed for stability
Swapping with the right child even when the left child is smaller, because only one of the two children was considered
Off-by-one in `right = 2*i + 2` check (`<` vs `<=`) causing an out-of-bounds or missed child

description:
Bug report: Our canonical Huffman decoder over-reads into the padding bits at the end of the encoded byte array, emitting spurious extra symbols whenever the logical bit-length is NOT a multiple of 8.

For example, decoding `['a', 'b', 'c']` with our table produces bits `0 10 110` (6 bits), then the final byte is padded with 2 zero bits (`01011000` = `0x58`). Our decoder emits an extra `'a'` after reading the first padding bit (because `0` is `'a'`'s code). It should stop exactly at bit 6.

The decoder takes a code table (`{symbol: bitstring}`, where `bitstring` is a str of `'0'`/`'1'`) and a `bytes` object. We pass the total bit length explicitly because the byte boundary does not have to coincide with the last code's boundary.

Here is the current implementation that has bugs:

```python
def build_decode_tree(code_table):
    """Build a binary tree: each internal node is a 2-list [left, right];
    each leaf is the symbol (any object).
    """
    root = [None, None]
    for sym, bits in code_table.items():
        node = root
        for b in bits[:-1]:
            idx = int(b)
            if node[idx] is None:
                node[idx] = [None, None]
            node = node[idx]
        last = int(bits[-1])
        if node[last] is not None:
            raise ValueError(f'Prefix collision at code {bits!r}')
        node[last] = sym
    return root


def huffman_decode(code_table, data, bit_length):
    """Decode a bit-stream (stored as `data` bytes, with `bit_length` bits total,
    MSB-first within each byte) into a list of symbols using the canonical
    Huffman code table.
    """
    root = build_decode_tree(code_table)
    out = []
    node = root
    consumed = 0
    for byte in data:
        for shift in range(7, -1, -1):
            if consumed > bit_length:
                return out
            bit = (byte >> shift) & 1
            node = node[bit]
            if not isinstance(node, list):
                out.append(node)
                node = root
            consumed += 1
    return out
```

Please fix the bug so all tests below pass. Do not change the function signatures or the MSB-first byte layout.
test_code:
# Canonical code table: a=0, b=10, c=110, d=111
table = {'a': '0', 'b': '10', 'c': '110', 'd': '111'}


def encode(symbols, table):
    bits = ''.join(table[s] for s in symbols)
    blen = len(bits)
    # pad to byte boundary with 0s
    pad = (-blen) % 8
    bits += '0' * pad
    out = bytearray()
    for i in range(0, len(bits), 8):
        out.append(int(bits[i:i + 8], 2))
    return bytes(out), blen


# Test 1: Short sequence
syms = ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']
data, blen = encode(syms, table)
assert huffman_decode(table, data, blen) == syms

# Test 2: Bit-length is exact multiple of 8 — exposes the final-symbol bug
syms = ['a'] * 8  # 8 bits exactly
data, blen = encode(syms, table)
assert blen % 8 == 0
res = huffman_decode(table, data, blen)
assert res == syms, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 3: Ends exactly on byte boundary with a multi-bit code
syms = ['b', 'b', 'b', 'b']  # 10 10 10 10 = 8 bits
data, blen = encode(syms, table)
assert blen == 8
res = huffman_decode(table, data, blen)
assert res == syms, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: Ends mid-byte (non-multiple of 8)
syms = ['a', 'b', 'c']  # 0 10 110 = 6 bits
data, blen = encode(syms, table)
assert blen == 6
res = huffman_decode(table, data, blen)
assert res == syms, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: Single symbol
syms = ['d']
data, blen = encode(syms, table)
res = huffman_decode(table, data, blen)
assert res == syms

# Test 6: Prefix-collision table raises
try:
    build_decode_tree({'x': '0', 'y': '00'})
    assert False, 'Expected ValueError for prefix collision'
except ValueError:
    pass

# Test 7: Larger alphabet, exact byte boundary
big_table = {'A': '00', 'B': '01', 'C': '10', 'D': '11'}
syms = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'A', 'B', 'C', 'D']  # 16 bits
data, blen = encode(syms, big_table)
assert blen == 16
res = huffman_decode(big_table, data, blen)
assert res == syms, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 8: Table with single symbol code '0' only
solo = {'z': '0'}
syms = ['z'] * 16  # 16 bits total
data = bytes([0, 0])
res = huffman_decode(solo, data, 16)
assert res == syms

print('All huffman_decode tests passed!')
common_bugs: Using `>` instead of `>=` in the bit-length termination check, so one extra bit past the end is read from the padding
Placing the length check after the emit (so the spurious bit can finalise a leaf) rather than before bits are read
Confusing MSB-first with LSB-first: reading bits as `byte & (1 << shift)` without the final `!= 0` gives a non-boolean, and reading as `(byte >> (7 - shift)) & 1` reverses the order
Resetting `node = root` only on the leaf branch but leaving stale state when a new byte begins mid-code

description:
Bug report: Our fixed-capacity ring buffer with overwrite-on-full silently loses data on read after an overwrite. When the buffer is full and a new write overwrites the oldest slot, the read pointer is *not* advanced; on the next read, we return the element that was just overwritten (i.e. the new value, not the expected old one) and worse, subsequent reads are off by one.

Correct semantics:
  - `write(x)` always stores `x`. If the buffer is full, it advances the read pointer too, so the new `x` replaces the oldest still-unread element.
  - `read()` returns the next unread element, or raises `IndexError` if empty.
  - `len(buf)` is the number of currently unread elements, always between 0 and `capacity`.

Here is the current implementation that has bugs:

```python
class RingBuffer:
    """Fixed-capacity single-reader single-writer ring buffer.
    Writes never block; when full, the oldest element is dropped (overwrite).
    """

    def __init__(self, capacity):
        if capacity <= 0:
            raise ValueError('capacity must be positive')
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.buf = [None] * capacity
        self.r = 0
        self.w = 0
        self.size = 0

    def write(self, x):
        self.buf[self.w] = x
        self.w = (self.w + 1) % self.capacity
        if self.size < self.capacity:
            self.size += 1
        # Bug: when full, we overwrote buf[w] but did not advance r,
        # so the read pointer now points at the freshly written slot.

    def read(self):
        if self.size == 0:
            raise IndexError('read from empty buffer')
        x = self.buf[self.r]
        self.r = (self.r + 1) % self.capacity
        self.size -= 1
        return x

    def peek(self):
        if self.size == 0:
            raise IndexError('peek on empty buffer')
        return self.buf[self.r]

    def __len__(self):
        return self.size
```

Please fix the bug so the overwrite branch advances the read pointer correctly.
test_code:
# Test 1: Simple FIFO within capacity
rb = RingBuffer(3)
rb.write(1)
rb.write(2)
rb.write(3)
assert len(rb) == 3
assert rb.read() == 1
assert rb.read() == 2
assert rb.read() == 3
assert len(rb) == 0

# Test 2: Empty read raises
rb = RingBuffer(3)
try:
    rb.read()
    assert False, 'Expected IndexError'
except IndexError:
    pass

# Test 3: Overwrite drops oldest — the key fix
rb = RingBuffer(3)
rb.write(1)
rb.write(2)
rb.write(3)
rb.write(4)  # overwrites 1
assert len(rb) == 3
assert rb.read() == 2
assert rb.read() == 3
assert rb.read() == 4
assert len(rb) == 0

# Test 4: Multiple overwrites in a row
rb = RingBuffer(2)
for x in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]:
    rb.write(x)
assert len(rb) == 2
assert rb.read() == 4
assert rb.read() == 5

# Test 5: Interleaved read/write
rb = RingBuffer(3)
rb.write(1); rb.write(2)
assert rb.read() == 1
rb.write(3); rb.write(4)
assert rb.read() == 2
rb.write(5); rb.write(6)  # now: 3,4,5,6 attempted; capacity 3 — overwrites 3
assert len(rb) == 3
assert rb.read() == 4
assert rb.read() == 5
assert rb.read() == 6

# Test 6: Peek doesn't consume
rb = RingBuffer(3)
rb.write('a'); rb.write('b')
assert rb.peek() == 'a'
assert rb.peek() == 'a'
assert len(rb) == 2

# Test 7: Peek on empty raises
rb = RingBuffer(2)
try:
    rb.peek()
    assert False, 'Expected IndexError'
except IndexError:
    pass

# Test 8: Capacity 1 (degenerate)
rb = RingBuffer(1)
rb.write('x')
rb.write('y')  # overwrites 'x'
assert rb.read() == 'y'
assert len(rb) == 0

# Test 9: Invalid capacity
try:
    RingBuffer(0)
    assert False
except ValueError:
    pass

# Test 10: Fill, drain, fill, drain — pointers wrap
rb = RingBuffer(4)
for cycle in range(3):
    for x in range(cycle * 10, cycle * 10 + 4):
        rb.write(x)
    for x in range(cycle * 10, cycle * 10 + 4):
        assert rb.read() == x

print('All RingBuffer tests passed!')
common_bugs: Advancing only the write pointer on overwrite, leaving the read pointer on the just-overwritten slot
Incrementing `size` beyond capacity on overwrite instead of keeping it clamped
Advancing the read pointer unconditionally (even when not full), corrupting reads for the non-overwrite case
Using `self.w == self.r` as the fullness test without distinguishing empty vs full

description:
Bug report: Our grapheme-aware string truncator splits multi-codepoint graphemes in the middle. We truncate based on `len(s)` (code-point count), so a truncation landing inside a ZWJ sequence (e.g. `'👨\u200d👩\u200d👧'`, a family emoji built from three people joined by zero-width joiners) produces an orphaned ZWJ at the end, or an incomplete cluster.

For this task, a grapheme cluster is defined by these simplified rules (subset of UAX #29 sufficient to pass the tests):
  1. A base character starts a new cluster.
  2. Any combining mark (Unicode category beginning with `M`, i.e. `Mn`, `Mc`, `Me`) extends the current cluster.
  3. A Zero-Width Joiner (`U+200D`) inside a cluster joins the next character into the same cluster (provided the next character exists).
  4. A Regional Indicator (`U+1F1E6..U+1F1FF`) pairs with the following Regional Indicator if one immediately follows (country flags).
  5. A Variation Selector (`U+FE00..U+FE0F`) extends the current cluster.

`truncate_graphemes(s, n)` must return the prefix of `s` containing at most `n` complete grapheme clusters (never a partial one), with no trailing orphan combining marks, ZWJs, or variation selectors.

Here is the current implementation that has a bug:

```python
import unicodedata

def truncate_graphemes(s, n):
    """Truncate `s` to at most `n` grapheme clusters. Never split a cluster."""
    if n <= 0:
        return ''
    return s[:n]
```

Please replace the body so the function actually respects grapheme boundaries per the rules above.
test_code:
# Test 1: Pure ASCII
assert truncate_graphemes('hello world', 5) == 'hello'
assert truncate_graphemes('hello', 100) == 'hello'
assert truncate_graphemes('hello', 0) == ''

# Test 2: Combining marks extend the base
# é as 'e' + U+0301 should be one grapheme
s = 'e\u0301llo'  # e+acute, then llo
assert truncate_graphemes(s, 1) == 'e\u0301'
assert truncate_graphemes(s, 2) == 'e\u0301l'

# Test 3: ZWJ sequence — family emoji, 5 codepoints, 1 grapheme
fam = '\U0001F468\u200d\U0001F469\u200d\U0001F467'  # man+ZWJ+woman+ZWJ+girl
assert truncate_graphemes(fam, 1) == fam
assert truncate_graphemes(fam + 'x', 1) == fam
assert truncate_graphemes('a' + fam + 'b', 2) == 'a' + fam
assert truncate_graphemes('a' + fam + 'b', 3) == 'a' + fam + 'b'

# Test 4: Truncation must not leave a trailing ZWJ
res = truncate_graphemes('a' + fam, 1)
assert res == 'a'
assert not res.endswith('\u200d')

# Test 5: Regional indicators pair up into flags
# US flag = U+1F1FA + U+1F1F8
flag_us = '\U0001F1FA\U0001F1F8'
flag_jp = '\U0001F1EF\U0001F1F5'
s = flag_us + flag_jp + 'x'
assert truncate_graphemes(s, 1) == flag_us
assert truncate_graphemes(s, 2) == flag_us + flag_jp
assert truncate_graphemes(s, 3) == flag_us + flag_jp + 'x'

# Test 6: Unpaired regional indicator counts as one grapheme on its own
lone_ri = '\U0001F1FA' + 'x'
assert truncate_graphemes(lone_ri, 1) == '\U0001F1FA'
assert truncate_graphemes(lone_ri, 2) == lone_ri

# Test 7: Variation selector extends base
# Heart with VS16 emoji presentation: U+2764 U+FE0F
heart_emoji = '\u2764\ufe0f'
assert truncate_graphemes(heart_emoji + 'x', 1) == heart_emoji
res = truncate_graphemes(heart_emoji + 'x', 1)
assert not res.endswith('\ufe0f') or res == heart_emoji

# Test 8: Mixed content
mixed = 'hi' + fam + '!' + flag_us + 'end'
assert truncate_graphemes(mixed, 4) == 'hi' + fam + '!'
assert truncate_graphemes(mixed, 5) == 'hi' + fam + '!' + flag_us

# Test 9: n larger than total grapheme count returns full string
assert truncate_graphemes('a\u0301bc', 99) == 'a\u0301bc'

# Test 10: Truncation never ends with an orphan ZWJ or combining mark
for k in range(0, 10):
    r = truncate_graphemes('a' + fam + 'b' + flag_us, k)
    if r:
        last = r[-1]
        cat = unicodedata.category(last)
        assert last != '\u200d', f'k={k}: ends with ZWJ: {r!r}'
        assert not cat.startswith('M'), f'k={k}: ends with combining mark: {r!r}'

print('All truncate_graphemes tests passed!')
common_bugs: Slicing by code-point count instead of grapheme-cluster count
Treating ZWJ as a cluster break when it should be a joiner extending the current cluster
Not special-casing Regional Indicator pairs, so a flag counts as two graphemes
Leaving a trailing ZWJ or combining mark at the cut point when the cluster is incomplete

description:
Bug report: Our cron-field parser miscomputes step ranges. For a single cron field (one of minute 0-59, hour 0-23, dom 1-31, month 1-12, dow 0-6) the grammar we support is:

  - `*` — every valid value
  - `a-b` — range `a..b` inclusive
  - `a-b/s` — range `a..b` inclusive, stepping by `s`
  - `*/s` — every `s`th value starting at `lo`
  - `a/s` — from `a` to `hi` inclusive, stepping by `s`
  - `v,w,x` — comma-separated union of the above
  - `v` — a single value

The values `lo` and `hi` are the field's inclusive bounds.

Expected behaviour:
  - `parse_cron_field('*/5', 0, 59)` → `[0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55]`
  - `parse_cron_field('10-30/5', 0, 59)` → `[10, 15, 20, 25, 30]`
  - `parse_cron_field('1-10/3', 0, 59)` → `[1, 4, 7, 10]`
  - `parse_cron_field('0,15,30,45', 0, 59)` → `[0, 15, 30, 45]`
  - `parse_cron_field('55/10', 0, 59)` → `[55]`
  - Out-of-range or malformed input should raise `ValueError`.

Our parser has two bugs:
  1. It drops the upper bound of an `a-b/s` range when `(b - a) % s == 0` (off-by-one in the `range()` stop).
  2. For `a/s` (no explicit upper), it walks from `a` but uses `hi` (not `hi + 1`) as the `range()` stop — so `55/10` gives `[]` instead of `[55]`.

Here is the current implementation that has bugs:

```python
def parse_cron_field(spec, lo, hi):
    """Parse a single cron field into a sorted, deduplicated list of ints."""
    out = set()
    for part in spec.split(','):
        if not part:
            raise ValueError(f'empty part in {spec!r}')
        step = 1
        if '/' in part:
            base, step_s = part.split('/', 1)
            step = int(step_s)
            if step < 1:
                raise ValueError(f'step must be >= 1 in {part!r}')
        else:
            base = part
        if base == '*':
            a, b = lo, hi
        elif '-' in base:
            a_s, b_s = base.split('-', 1)
            a, b = int(a_s), int(b_s)
        else:
            a = int(base)
            b = a if step == 1 else hi
        if a < lo or b > hi or a > b:
            raise ValueError(f'out-of-range in {part!r} (bounds {lo}..{hi})')
        # Bug: stop is `b` (exclusive), so values equal to b are dropped.
        for v in range(a, b, step):
            out.add(v)
    return sorted(out)
```

Please fix the bugs so all tests pass.
test_code:
# Test 1: Star/step
assert parse_cron_field('*/5', 0, 59) == [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55]

# Test 2: Range/step with inclusive upper
assert parse_cron_field('10-30/5', 0, 59) == [10, 15, 20, 25, 30]

# Test 3: Range/step where (b-a) % s != 0 (upper not reachable)
assert parse_cron_field('1-10/3', 0, 59) == [1, 4, 7, 10]

# Test 4: Comma-separated union
assert parse_cron_field('0,15,30,45', 0, 59) == [0, 15, 30, 45]

# Test 5: a/s — single start with step, must reach hi inclusive
assert parse_cron_field('55/10', 0, 59) == [55]
assert parse_cron_field('0/10', 0, 59) == [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
assert parse_cron_field('1/2', 1, 7) == [1, 3, 5, 7]

# Test 6: Plain star
assert parse_cron_field('*', 0, 6) == [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

# Test 7: Plain range
assert parse_cron_field('2-5', 0, 10) == [2, 3, 4, 5]

# Test 8: Single value
assert parse_cron_field('3', 0, 10) == [3]

# Test 9: Deduplication across commas
assert parse_cron_field('0-4,2-6', 0, 10) == [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

# Test 10: Out-of-range
for bad in ['60', '5-70', '-1', '100', '']:
    try:
        parse_cron_field(bad, 0, 59)
        assert False, f'Expected ValueError on {bad!r}'
    except ValueError:
        pass

# Test 11: Inverted range
try:
    parse_cron_field('10-5', 0, 59)
    assert False, 'Expected ValueError on inverted range'
except ValueError:
    pass

# Test 12: Step of zero or negative
for bad in ['*/0', '5-10/0', '*/-2']:
    try:
        parse_cron_field(bad, 0, 59)
        assert False, f'Expected ValueError on {bad!r}'
    except ValueError:
        pass

# Test 13: Comma with single value adjacent to range
assert parse_cron_field('5,10-12,59', 0, 59) == [5, 10, 11, 12, 59]

print('All parse_cron_field tests passed!')
common_bugs: Using `range(a, b, step)` (exclusive stop) when the upper bound must be inclusive
Falling back to `hi` as the upper bound for `a/s` but then stopping at `hi` exclusive, never producing `hi`
Treating `a/s` with `a == hi` as empty instead of `[hi]`
Not validating that step >= 1 or that bounds are respected after normalisation

test_code:
# Test 1: Simple — only one option matches
assert select_media_type('application/json', ['application/json', 'text/html']) == 'application/json'

# Test 2: Default q = 1.0 beats lower explicit q
res = select_media_type('text/html, application/json; q=0.9', ['application/json', 'text/html'])
assert res == 'text/html', f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 3: Wildcard default q matches but concrete type wins by specificity
res = select_media_type('text/html; q=0.5, */*', ['text/html', 'application/json'])
# text/html: specificity 2, q=0.5. application/json: specificity 0, q=1.0.
# application/json wins (higher q).
assert res == 'application/json', f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: Ties broken by specificity
res = select_media_type('text/*; q=0.8, text/html; q=0.8', ['text/plain', 'text/html'])
# text/plain matches text/*  at q=0.8 (specificity 1)
# text/html matches text/html at q=0.8 (specificity 2)
assert res == 'text/html', f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: q=0 means explicit refusal
res = select_media_type('text/html; q=0, application/json; q=0.5', ['text/html'])
assert res is None, f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 6: q=0 on one, default on another
res = select_media_type('text/html; q=0, application/json', ['text/html', 'application/json'])
assert res == 'application/json', f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 7: No matching server offer
res = select_media_type('image/png', ['text/html', 'application/json'])
assert res is None

# Test 8: Server-preference tiebreak when q and specificity both tie
res = select_media_type('text/html, application/json', ['application/json', 'text/html'])
# Both q=1.0, both specificity 2 — server prefers application/json
assert res == 'application/json', f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 9: */* only, pick first server offer
res = select_media_type('*/*', ['application/json', 'text/html'])
assert res == 'application/json'

# Test 10: Parameter on media type should not break parsing
res = select_media_type('text/html; level=1; q=0.8, text/html; q=0.4', ['text/html'])
assert res == 'text/html'

# Test 11: Empty available list
assert select_media_type('*/*', []) is None

# Test 12: Complex — three offers, three accepts
res = select_media_type(
    'text/*; q=0.5, application/json; q=0.9, */*; q=0.1',
    ['text/html', 'application/json', 'application/xml']
)
# text/html: text/* specificity 1, q=0.5
# application/json: concrete 2, q=0.9
# application/xml: only */* matches, q=0.1
# winner: application/json
assert res == 'application/json', f'Got {res!r}'

print('All select_media_type tests passed!')
common_bugs: Defaulting q to 0.0 when the `q=` parameter is absent, instead of 1.0 as required by RFC 7231
Sorting the matches list with `reverse=True` but then taking the last element, yielding the least specific match
Using `>` instead of `>=` combined with an inverted tiebreak policy, so later server offers unfairly beat earlier ones
Not stripping `;`-separated media-type parameters (e.g. `level=1`) before parsing the subtype
